---
title: "Rank and the Four Fundamental Subspaces"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / data
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # the highlighted vector / image / column space
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # null space / orthogonality / residual highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the capstone of **Movement III — Matrices as
> Maps**. It assumes [GEOM-02 *Span and Subspaces*] (a subspace is the set of all linear
> combinations of some vectors — a line or plane through the origin, always containing
> $\mathbf{0}$), [GEOM-03 *Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension*] (a basis is a
> minimal spanning set, *dimension* is the provably invariant count of basis vectors, and
> "independent columns" means no redundancy), [GEOM-04 *The Dot Product*] (the dot product
> $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}$, its linearity, and perpendicularity as $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}=0$),
> [GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection*] (in
> particular the **orthogonal complement** — every vector splits uniquely into a part in a
> subspace and a part perpendicular to it), [GEOM-08 *Matrices as Linear Maps*]
> ($A\mathbf{x}$ is a linear combination of $A$'s columns), and [GEOM-11 *Solving
> $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$*] (when the system has a unique solution, no solution, or
> infinitely many). It leans on the transpose $A^\top$ from [GEOM-10]. Its job is to
> answer one question — *how much real dimensionality does a matrix carry?* — by naming the
> single number, the **rank**, and the four subspaces that organize everything a matrix
> does. It sets up [GEOM-14]–[GEOM-15] (eigenstructure), is read off effortlessly from the
> **SVD** in [GEOM-20], and is the geometry behind the **condition number** of [GEOM-26].
> Most directly, *near*-rank-deficiency is **multicollinearity**, and this article is the
> linear-algebra core that **Regression R5/R7** stand on.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A matrix $A$ has as many columns as it has, and as many rows — but those counts lie about
how much it actually *does*. A $3\times 3$ matrix whose three columns all point along one
line moves space onto a line: it has the reach of a single vector, not three. The honest
measure of that reach is the **rank**: the dimension of the space the columns can build.
This article defines rank, proves the fact that makes it a single well-defined number —
**row rank equals column rank** — and then lays out the four subspaces every matrix
$A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ carries: the **column space** and **left null space** living
in the output space $\mathbb{R}^m$, the **row space** and **null space** living in the
input space $\mathbb{R}^n$. We derive the **rank–nullity theorem** — that the dimensions
of the null space and the column space must add up to $n$ — and we prove the
orthogonality that pins the four subspaces into two perpendicular pairs. The single claim
this article defends is that **these four subspaces are not four facts to memorize but
one picture**: a matrix takes its $n$-dimensional input space, collapses a null-space
slab to zero, and maps what survives one-to-one onto its column space — and *rank* is the
dimension of what survives.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **rank** of a matrix is the dimension of the space its columns can reach — the number
of genuinely independent directions among them. It equals the dimension of the space its
*rows* span, so there is just one number. A matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ then sorts
all of space into **four subspaces**: in the input $\mathbb{R}^n$, the **row space** (what
the matrix "sees" and acts on, dimension $r$) and the **null space** (what it crushes to
zero, dimension $n-r$), and these two are *orthogonal complements*; in the output
$\mathbb{R}^m$, the **column space** (everything reachable, dimension $r$) and the **left
null space** (everything missed, dimension $m-r$), again orthogonal complements. The whole
story is one sentence: *a matrix maps its row space one-to-one onto its column space and
sends everything perpendicular to the row space to zero.*
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The two equalities it asserts — that the column space
and row space have the *same* dimension $r$, and that the null space takes up exactly the
$n-r$ dimensions the row space leaves behind — look like coincidences. We will see they
are forced: there is exactly $n$ dimensions of input, the matrix can only act
nontrivially along $r$ of them, and whatever is left over has nowhere to go but zero.

# Why we are doing this: the problem of phantom dimensionality {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **a matrix announces a size — $m$ rows,
$n$ columns — but how much does it actually do, and where does the rest go?** Every
definition and proof below is an answer to some piece of that.

## The over-counting problem

By [GEOM-08], a matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ is a map $\mathbf{x}\mapsto A\mathbf{x}$
from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^m$, and $A\mathbf{x}$ is a **linear combination of the
columns of $A$**, with the entries of $\mathbf{x}$ as the weights. Write the columns as
$\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n\in\mathbb{R}^m$; then

$$
A\mathbf{x}
= x_1\mathbf{a}_1 + x_2\mathbf{a}_2 + \dots + x_n\mathbf{a}_n .
$$ {#eq-Ax-as-combo}

The set of *all* outputs $A\mathbf{x}$ as $\mathbf{x}$ ranges over $\mathbb{R}^n$ is
therefore the set of all linear combinations of the columns — their **span** (from
[GEOM-02]). Now the danger. The matrix carries $n$ columns, but the columns may carry
*less* information than their count suggests. Suppose

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ -1 & -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

Three columns, three rows — it *looks* three-dimensional. But the second column is
$2\times$ the first and the third is $3\times$ the first: all three point along the single
direction $(1,2,-1)$. No matter what $\mathbf{x}$ you feed in, $A\mathbf{x}$ lands
somewhere on the line through $(1,2,-1)$. This $3\times3$ matrix has the reach of *one*
vector. Its nine numbers are mostly redundant; its true dimensionality is $1$.

This is not a contrived pathology. In data, columns are features and rows are
observations. If "height in centimeters" and "height in inches" are both in your design
matrix, one column is a scalar multiple of the other and the matrix is rank-deficient — it
*looks* like it carries two features' worth of information but carries one. More subtly, if
"total income" is the sum of "wage income" and "investment income," that exact linear
dependence makes the design matrix rank-deficient, and the consequences ripple straight
into regression: $X^\top X$ becomes non-invertible and the coefficients are no longer
determined (the multicollinearity story of **Regression R7**). The honest measure of "how
many features' worth of independent information is really here" is the rank.

## Why four subspaces, and not just one

If a matrix's reach is smaller than its size, two questions immediately follow, and they
are the questions the four subspaces answer:

1. **What can it reach?** The outputs $A\mathbf{x}$ fill out a subspace of
   $\mathbb{R}^m$ — the **column space**. Equally important is its complement: which target
   vectors $\mathbf{b}$ are *unreachable*, so that $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has no solution
   (the "no solution" case of [GEOM-11])? That unreachable direction is governed by the
   **left null space**.
2. **What does it lose?** If the columns are redundant, different inputs $\mathbf{x}$
   produce the *same* output, so information is destroyed. The inputs that get crushed all
   the way to $\mathbf{0}$ form the **null space**; the inputs the matrix faithfully
   distinguishes form the **row space**.

These four — column space, null space, row space, left null space — are Gilbert Strang's
"four fundamental subspaces," and his insistence on teaching them as *one diagram* rather
than four definitions is the spirit of this article. Once you see the picture (@fig-bigpic
at the end), the rank–nullity theorem stops being a formula to recall and becomes
something you can read off the diagram.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does the rank of a data matrix tell you, in one sentence?"* It tells you the number
of genuinely independent directions of variation among your columns (features) — or
equivalently among your rows (observations), since the two counts are equal. A rank far
below the number of columns means your features are partly redundant; the data lives in a
lower-dimensional flat than the column count advertises. That single observation is the
seed of dimensionality reduction (PCA, owned by Unsupervised Learning, built on the SVD of
[GEOM-20]) and the diagnosis of multicollinearity in regression.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any proofs, fix the picture of what a matrix *does*, because all four subspaces are
just features of that one picture.

A matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ takes an arrow $\mathbf{x}$ living in the input room
$\mathbb{R}^n$ and produces an arrow $A\mathbf{x}$ in the output room $\mathbb{R}^m$. Watch
what happens as $\mathbf{x}$ roams over the *entire* input room. Two things occur at once.

**Some input directions get flattened to nothing.** If the columns are redundant, there are
directions $\mathbf{x}\neq\mathbf{0}$ with $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$ — the matrix sends them
to the origin, erasing them. Gather every such direction and you get a flat slab through
the origin of the input room: the **null space**. The matrix is blind to it; any movement
along that slab produces no movement at all in the output.

**What survives lands inside a smaller-or-equal flat in the output room.** The reachable
outputs cannot fill more of $\mathbb{R}^m$ than the columns can build. They fill a flat
through the origin — the **column space**. Its dimension is the rank: the number of
independent directions that made it through.

@fig-collapse shows the cleanest case to hold in your head: a $2\times2$ matrix of rank
$1$. The whole plane is squashed onto a single line (the column space), and a whole line of
inputs (the null space) is crushed to the origin.

```{python}
#| label: fig-collapse
#| fig-cap: 'A rank-1 map (the matrix with rows $(1,2)$ and $(2,4)$) acting on the plane. **Left:** the input room $\mathbb{R}^2$; the green line is the null space (every input on it maps to $\mathbf{0}$). **Right:** the output room $\mathbb{R}^2$; the entire plane collapses onto the red line — the column space, spanned by $(1,2)$. Two genuinely different inputs (blue arrows) land on the *same* output if they differ by a null-space vector. Rank is the dimension of the surviving line: $1$.'
A = np.array([[1.0, 2.0], [2.0, 4.0]])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))

# Left: input room with null space.
# Null space of [[1,2],[2,4]]: x1 + 2 x2 = 0  => direction (2,-1) (since 2 - 2 = 0).
t = np.linspace(-2.4, 2.4, 50)
nulldir = np.array([2.0, -1.0]) / np.sqrt(5)
axL.plot(t * nulldir[0] * 2.2, t * nulldir[1] * 2.2, color=ACCENT, lw=2.5,
         label="null space")
for v, c in [((2.0, 0.5), VEC), ((-1.0, 1.5), PALE)]:
    draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), v, color=c)
axL.scatter([0], [0], color="k", s=18, zorder=6)
axL.set_xlim(-3, 3); axL.set_ylim(-3, 3); axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_title("Input room $\\mathbb{R}^2$"); axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$")

# Right: output room with column space (line through (1,2)).
coldir = np.array([1.0, 2.0]) / np.sqrt(5)
axR.plot(t * coldir[0] * 2.6, t * coldir[1] * 2.6, color=HILITE, lw=2.5,
         label="column space")
for v, c in [((2.0, 0.5), VEC), ((-1.0, 1.5), PALE)]:
    Av = A @ np.array(v)
    draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), tuple(Av), color=c)
axR.scatter([0], [0], color="k", s=18, zorder=6)
axR.set_xlim(-7, 7); axR.set_ylim(-7, 7); axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_title("Output room $\\mathbb{R}^2$"); axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9)
axR.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y_2$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The two remaining subspaces are the perpendicular partners of these two. The **row space**
is what is left in the input room *after* you remove the null slab — it is the part of the
input the matrix genuinely responds to, and (we will prove) it sits exactly perpendicular
to the null space. The **left null space** is what is left in the output room after you
remove the column space — the directions in $\mathbb{R}^m$ the matrix can never reach, sitting
perpendicular to the column space. Hold this single image — *crush a slab, map the rest
faithfully onto a flat* — and the formal development is bookkeeping on top of it.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We build in four moves: define the spaces (@sec-defs), prove rank is one number
(@sec-rank-equal), prove rank–nullity (@sec-ranknullity), and prove the orthogonality that
pairs the subspaces (@sec-ortho).

## The four subspaces, defined {#sec-defs}

Throughout, $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ has columns $\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n\in
\mathbb{R}^m$ and rows $\mathbf{r}_1^\top,\dots,\mathbf{r}_m^\top$, where each
$\mathbf{r}_i\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is the $i$-th row written as a column vector.

::: {#def-colspace}
**Definition (column space).** The **column space** of $A$, written $C(A)$, is the span of
its columns:
$$
C(A) = \{A\mathbf{x} : \mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n\}
     = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m .
$$
:::

The two expressions are the same set by @eq-Ax-as-combo: the outputs $A\mathbf{x}$ are
exactly the linear combinations of the columns. $C(A)$ is a genuine subspace of
$\mathbb{R}^m$ — it is a span, and a span is closed under addition and scaling and contains
$\mathbf{0}$ (the empty/zero combination), the defining properties of a subspace from
[GEOM-02]. The column space is **the set of reachable outputs**: $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has
a solution if and only if $\mathbf{b}\in C(A)$.

::: {#def-nullspace}
**Definition (null space).** The **null space** of $A$, written $N(A)$, is the set of inputs
the matrix sends to zero:
$$
N(A) = \{\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n : A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n .
$$
:::

That $N(A)$ is a subspace needs one line, and we give it rather than assert it. Suppose
$\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\in N(A)$ and $c$ is a scalar. Then by linearity of the map
(proved in [GEOM-08]: $A(\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v})=A\mathbf{u}+A\mathbf{v}$ and
$A(c\mathbf{u})=cA\mathbf{u}$),
$$
A(\mathbf{u}+c\mathbf{v}) = A\mathbf{u} + cA\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} + c\mathbf{0}
= \mathbf{0},
$$
so $\mathbf{u}+c\mathbf{v}\in N(A)$. Closed under addition and scaling, and it contains
$\mathbf{0}$ (since $A\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}$) — a subspace. The null space is **the inputs
the matrix is blind to**: it is exactly the directions you can add to any solution of
$A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ without changing the output, which is why a nontrivial null space
means *infinitely many* solutions (the third case of [GEOM-11]).

The remaining two subspaces are the same constructions applied to the transpose $A^\top$,
whose columns are the rows of $A$ (the transpose flips rows and columns — [GEOM-10]).

::: {#def-rowspace}
**Definition (row space).** The **row space** of $A$ is the span of its rows — equivalently,
the column space of $A^\top$:
$$
C(A^\top) = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{r}_1,\dots,\mathbf{r}_m\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n .
$$
:::

::: {#def-leftnull}
**Definition (left null space).** The **left null space** of $A$ is the null space of
$A^\top$:
$$
N(A^\top) = \{\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^m : A^\top\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{0}\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m .
$$
The name comes from transposing the condition: $A^\top\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{0}$ is the same as
$\mathbf{y}^\top A=\mathbf{0}^\top$ (transpose both sides, using $(A^\top\mathbf{y})^\top
=\mathbf{y}^\top A$ from [GEOM-10]), i.e. $\mathbf{y}$ multiplies $A$ *from the left* to
give zero.
:::

Notice where each lives. $C(A)$ and $N(A^\top)$ live in the **output** room $\mathbb{R}^m$
(they are about $m$-vectors). $N(A)$ and $C(A^\top)$ live in the **input** room
$\mathbb{R}^n$. Two subspaces per room — and within each room they will turn out to be
perpendicular complements.

## Rank: column rank equals row rank {#sec-rank-equal}

The rank ought to be "the number of independent directions in $A$." But there are two
honest candidates — the dimension of the column space and the dimension of the row space —
and it is not obvious they agree. We define rank and then prove they do, so that "the rank"
is unambiguous.

::: {#def-rank}
**Definition (rank).** The **column rank** of $A$ is $\dim C(A)$, the number of vectors in a
basis of the column space. The **row rank** is $\dim C(A^\top)$, the dimension of the row
space. We call the common value (once proved equal) the **rank**, written $\operatorname{rank}(A)$ or $r$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why isn't the rank just the number of columns, or the number of rows?"* Because columns
can be redundant. The rank counts only the *independent* columns — the size of the largest
set of columns with no nontrivial linear combination equal to zero (the independence
condition of [GEOM-03]). For the matrix in @sec-why with all columns parallel, that largest
independent set has size $1$, so the rank is $1$ even though there are $3$ columns. Rank is
always at most $\min(m,n)$: you cannot have more independent columns than there are columns,
nor more than the dimension of the room $\mathbb{R}^m$ they live in.
:::

Now the theorem that makes "the rank" a single number. We prove it with a clean,
self-contained argument — a factorization that exhibits the same number $r$ on both sides.

::: {#thm-rank-equal}
**Theorem (row rank = column rank).** For every $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$,
$$
\dim C(A) = \dim C(A^\top).
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Let $r=\dim C(A)$ be the column rank. Pick a basis $\mathbf{c}_1,\dots,\mathbf{c}_r$ of the
column space $C(A)$ — that is $r$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^m$ that are independent and span
every column of $A$ (such a basis exists and has $r$ elements by the definition of
dimension, [GEOM-03]). Collect them as the columns of a matrix
$$
C = [\,\mathbf{c}_1 \;\; \mathbf{c}_2 \;\; \cdots \;\; \mathbf{c}_r\,]
\in \mathbb{R}^{m\times r}.
$$

Every column $\mathbf{a}_j$ of $A$ lives in $C(A)$, so it is some linear combination of the
basis vectors:
$$
\mathbf{a}_j = f_{1j}\mathbf{c}_1 + f_{2j}\mathbf{c}_2 + \dots + f_{rj}\mathbf{c}_r,
\qquad j=1,\dots,n,
$$
for scalars $f_{ij}$. Stack the coefficients of column $j$ into a vector
$\mathbf{f}_j=(f_{1j},\dots,f_{rj})^\top\in\mathbb{R}^r$. By @eq-Ax-as-combo read in
reverse, $\mathbf{a}_j = C\mathbf{f}_j$ (the combination of $C$'s columns with weights
$\mathbf{f}_j$). Assembling all $n$ columns side by side, with $F\in\mathbb{R}^{r\times n}$
the matrix whose $j$-th column is $\mathbf{f}_j$,
$$
A = C F . \tag{$\ast$}
$$
This is the key step: **every rank-$r$ matrix factors as (something $m\times r$) times
(something $r\times n$).** We have produced such a factorization with the column-space
basis.

Now read $(\ast)$ a second time, by **rows**. The transpose of a product reverses the order
(from [GEOM-10], $(CF)^\top = F^\top C^\top$), so $A^\top = F^\top C^\top$. Apply
@eq-Ax-as-combo's column-combination reading to $A^\top = F^\top C^\top$: each column of
$A^\top$ — that is, each *row of $A$* — is a linear combination of the columns of $F^\top$.
There are $r$ columns in $F^\top$ (since $F^\top\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times r}$). So the row
space of $A$, $C(A^\top)$, is spanned by $r$ vectors, which means
$$
\dim C(A^\top) \le r = \dim C(A).
$$
Every spanning set has at least as many vectors as the dimension ([GEOM-03]), so $r$
spanning vectors cannot force the dimension above $r$.

We have shown row rank $\le$ column rank for *every* matrix. Apply that very inequality to
$A^\top$ in place of $A$: its column rank is $\dim C(A^\top)$ and its row rank is
$\dim C\!\bigl((A^\top)^\top\bigr)=\dim C(A)$ (using $(A^\top)^\top=A$ from [GEOM-10]). The
inequality "row rank $\le$ column rank" applied to $A^\top$ reads
$$
\dim C(A) \le \dim C(A^\top).
$$
The two inequalities together force equality, $\dim C(A)=\dim C(A^\top)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So there is one rank $r$, and it is simultaneously the number of independent columns and
the number of independent rows. This is genuinely surprising the first time: the columns
live in $\mathbb{R}^m$ and the rows live in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — different rooms entirely — yet
they carry the same count of independent directions. The factorization $A=CF$ is the
reason, and it will reappear, in its most polished form, as the SVD ([GEOM-20]).

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me a quick way to see why row rank equals column rank without the factorization."*
The factorization *is* the quick way once you have it, but here is the row-reduction view
many courses use, stated so you can recognize it. Row operations (the moves of Gaussian
elimination, [GEOM-11]) never change the row space — each new row is a combination of old
rows — so they preserve row rank; and they never change which *combinations of columns* are
zero, so they preserve column rank too. Reduce $A$ to row-echelon form: the nonzero rows are
independent (row rank = number of pivots) and the pivot columns are independent and span the
rest (column rank = number of pivots). Same pivots, same count. The factorization proof
above is cleaner because it never mentions a particular elimination procedure — but if an
interviewer wants "the elimination argument," it is the pivot count.
:::

## The rank–nullity theorem {#sec-ranknullity}

We now connect the input-room subspaces. The matrix's $n$-dimensional input room splits
its dimensions between "directions the matrix crushes" (the null space) and "directions it
acts on faithfully" (which will turn out to be the row space). The accounting is exact.

::: {#thm-ranknullity}
**Theorem (rank–nullity).** For $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$,
$$
\dim C(A) + \dim N(A) = n .
$$
Writing $r=\operatorname{rank}(A)=\dim C(A)$ and calling $\dim N(A)$ the **nullity**, this
says
$$
\operatorname{rank}(A) + \operatorname{nullity}(A) = n
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
\dim N(A) = n - r .
$$
:::

The statement is "the dimensions of the surviving part and the crushed part add up to the
whole input room." We prove it by building an explicit basis of $\mathbb{R}^n$ that is
split cleanly into a null-space piece and a piece the matrix maps onto a column-space basis.

::: {.proof}
Let $k=\dim N(A)$ be the nullity. Choose a basis
$\mathbf{z}_1,\dots,\mathbf{z}_k$ of the null space $N(A)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ (these are
$k$ independent input directions, each crushed to zero). By the basis-extension fact from
[GEOM-03] — any independent set in a space can be enlarged to a basis of the whole space —
extend them to a basis of all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ by adjoining further vectors
$$
\mathbf{z}_1,\dots,\mathbf{z}_k,\ \mathbf{w}_1,\dots,\mathbf{w}_p ,
\qquad k + p = n,
$$
so $p = n-k$ is the number of "extra" directions we needed. We will show $p$ equals the
rank $r$, which gives $r + k = n$ — the theorem.

The plan: show that the images $A\mathbf{w}_1,\dots,A\mathbf{w}_p$ form a **basis of the
column space** $C(A)$. If they do, then $\dim C(A)=p$, i.e. $r=p$, and we are done.

**Step 1 — they span $C(A)$.** Take any output $\mathbf{b}\in C(A)$; then
$\mathbf{b}=A\mathbf{x}$ for some input $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$. Write $\mathbf{x}$ in
our basis:
$$
\mathbf{x} = c_1\mathbf{z}_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf{z}_k
           + d_1\mathbf{w}_1 + \dots + d_p\mathbf{w}_p .
$$
Apply $A$, using linearity ($A$ distributes over the sum and pulls out scalars, [GEOM-08]):
$$
\mathbf{b} = A\mathbf{x}
= \underbrace{c_1 A\mathbf{z}_1 + \dots + c_k A\mathbf{z}_k}_{\text{each }A\mathbf{z}_i=\mathbf{0}}
+ d_1 A\mathbf{w}_1 + \dots + d_p A\mathbf{w}_p .
$$
The $\mathbf{z}_i$ are in the null space, so every $A\mathbf{z}_i=\mathbf{0}$ and those
terms vanish. Hence $\mathbf{b}= d_1 A\mathbf{w}_1 + \dots + d_p A\mathbf{w}_p$ — a linear
combination of the $A\mathbf{w}_j$. So they span $C(A)$.

**Step 2 — they are independent.** Suppose some combination of them is zero:
$$
d_1 A\mathbf{w}_1 + \dots + d_p A\mathbf{w}_p = \mathbf{0}.
$$
Pull $A$ out front by linearity: $A\bigl(d_1\mathbf{w}_1+\dots+d_p\mathbf{w}_p\bigr)
=\mathbf{0}$. That says the vector $\mathbf{w}:=d_1\mathbf{w}_1+\dots+d_p\mathbf{w}_p$ is in
the null space $N(A)$. But $N(A)$ is spanned by the $\mathbf{z}_i$, so $\mathbf{w}$ is also
some combination of them:
$$
d_1\mathbf{w}_1+\dots+d_p\mathbf{w}_p = e_1\mathbf{z}_1+\dots+e_k\mathbf{z}_k
$$
for scalars $e_i$. Move everything to one side:
$$
d_1\mathbf{w}_1+\dots+d_p\mathbf{w}_p - e_1\mathbf{z}_1-\dots-e_k\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{0}.
$$
But $\mathbf{w}_1,\dots,\mathbf{w}_p,\mathbf{z}_1,\dots,\mathbf{z}_k$ are a *basis* of
$\mathbb{R}^n$, hence independent — the only combination equal to zero is the all-zeros one
(the independence condition of [GEOM-03]). Therefore every coefficient is zero; in
particular $d_1=\dots=d_p=0$. So the only way to combine the $A\mathbf{w}_j$ to zero is
trivially: they are independent.

Spanning (Step 1) and independent (Step 2) make $A\mathbf{w}_1,\dots,A\mathbf{w}_p$ a basis
of $C(A)$, so $\dim C(A)=p$. Combined with $k+p=n$ this gives
$\dim N(A)+\dim C(A)=k+p=n$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Read the proof's engine in plain words: the matrix maps the $p$ "extra" input directions
*one-to-one* onto a basis of its column space, while crushing the $k$ null directions to
nothing. So the column space's dimension $r$ is *literally* the number of input directions
that survive, and the null space's dimension $n-r$ is the number that die — they must add to
$n$ because every input direction does exactly one of the two. The theorem is conservation
of dimension: input dimensions are neither created nor destroyed, only sorted into
"survives" and "crushed."

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"A matrix is $5\times 8$ with rank $3$. Describe all four subspaces' dimensions."* Here
$m=5$, $n=8$, $r=3$. Column space: $\dim = r = 3$, inside $\mathbb{R}^5$. Null space:
$\dim = n-r = 8-3 = 5$, inside $\mathbb{R}^8$ (huge — five free directions, so
$A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has a $5$-dimensional family of solutions whenever it has any).
Row space: $\dim = r = 3$, inside $\mathbb{R}^8$. Left null space: $\dim = m-r = 5-3 = 2$,
inside $\mathbb{R}^5$ (two output directions are unreachable). Note the two dimensions in
the input room sum to $8=n$ (rank–nullity) and the two in the output room sum to $5=m$
(rank–nullity applied to $A^\top$). Being able to fill in this table on demand is the most
common exam question on this topic.
:::

Applying @thm-ranknullity to $A^\top$ (which is $n\times m$) gives the output-room
accounting: $\dim C(A^\top)+\dim N(A^\top)=m$. Since $\dim C(A^\top)=\dim C(A)=r$ by
@thm-rank-equal, the left null space has dimension $\dim N(A^\top)=m-r$. So all four
dimensions are determined by the three numbers $m,n,r$:

$$
\begin{array}{llll}
\dim C(A) = r, & \dim N(A^\top) = m - r & \quad(\text{output room } \mathbb{R}^m,\ \text{sum } m),\\[3pt]
\dim C(A^\top) = r, & \dim N(A) = n - r & \quad(\text{input room } \mathbb{R}^n,\ \text{sum } n).
\end{array}
$$ {#eq-four-dims}

## The orthogonality that pairs them {#sec-ortho}

The four dimensions in @eq-four-dims already pair up by room and sum to the room's
dimension. The final structural fact says the two subspaces in each room are not just
complementary in dimension but **perpendicular**: each is the *orthogonal complement* of
the other (the orthogonal complement $S^\perp$ of a subspace $S$ — every vector
perpendicular to all of $S$ — was introduced in [GEOM-06], where we proved every vector
splits uniquely into a part in $S$ and a part in $S^\perp$).

::: {#thm-ortho}
**Theorem (orthogonality of the subspaces).** For $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$:
$$
N(A) = C(A^\top)^\perp \quad\text{in } \mathbb{R}^n,
\qquad\qquad
N(A^\top) = C(A)^\perp \quad\text{in } \mathbb{R}^m.
$$
In words: the **null space is perpendicular to the row space**, and the **left null space is
perpendicular to the column space**.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the first; the second is the same statement applied to $A^\top$.

The whole proof hinges on one reading of matrix–vector multiplication, which we make
explicit. By the row-by-row view of $A\mathbf{x}$ (the second reading from [GEOM-08]: the
$i$-th entry of $A\mathbf{x}$ is the dot product of row $i$ with $\mathbf{x}$),
$$
A\mathbf{x} =
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{r}_1^\top\mathbf{x} \\ \mathbf{r}_2^\top\mathbf{x} \\ \vdots \\
\mathbf{r}_m^\top\mathbf{x} \end{bmatrix},
$$
where $\mathbf{r}_i$ is the $i$-th row of $A$ written as a column vector. So **$A\mathbf{x}
=\mathbf{0}$ means every row's dot product with $\mathbf{x}$ is zero**:
$$
A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathbf{r}_i^\top\mathbf{x}=0 \ \text{ for all } i=1,\dots,m.
$$ {#eq-null-rows}

Now we show the two sets $N(A)$ and $C(A^\top)^\perp$ are equal by showing each contains the
other.

*($N(A)\subseteq C(A^\top)^\perp$.)* Suppose $\mathbf{x}\in N(A)$. By @eq-null-rows,
$\mathbf{x}$ is perpendicular to every row $\mathbf{r}_i$. The row space $C(A^\top)$ is
spanned by the rows, so any element of it is a combination
$\mathbf{v}=\sum_i \alpha_i\mathbf{r}_i$. Then, using that the dot product distributes over
sums and pulls out scalars (linearity of the dot product, [GEOM-04]),
$$
\mathbf{v}^\top\mathbf{x}
= \Bigl(\sum_i \alpha_i\mathbf{r}_i\Bigr)^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}
= \sum_i \alpha_i\,\mathbf{r}_i^\top\mathbf{x}
= \sum_i \alpha_i\cdot 0 = 0 .
$$
So $\mathbf{x}$ is perpendicular to *every* vector in the row space, i.e.
$\mathbf{x}\in C(A^\top)^\perp$.

*($C(A^\top)^\perp\subseteq N(A)$.)* Conversely suppose $\mathbf{x}\in C(A^\top)^\perp$, so
$\mathbf{x}$ is perpendicular to everything in the row space. In particular it is
perpendicular to each row $\mathbf{r}_i$ (each row *is* in the row space), so
$\mathbf{r}_i^\top\mathbf{x}=0$ for all $i$. By @eq-null-rows that is exactly
$A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$, i.e. $\mathbf{x}\in N(A)$.

Each set contains the other, so $N(A)=C(A^\top)^\perp$. Replacing $A$ by $A^\top$ (whose
rows are the columns of $A$) gives $N(A^\top)=C(A)^\perp$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the deep payoff and it deserves a beat. The null space and the row space are not
two random subspaces of $\mathbb{R}^n$ that happen to have dimensions $n-r$ and $r$. They
are *perpendicular complements*: together they fill the whole input room with no overlap
except the origin, meeting at exactly $90^\circ$ (the complement decomposition of [GEOM-06]
guarantees $\dim C(A^\top)+\dim C(A^\top)^\perp = n$, which independently re-confirms
rank–nullity). Every input vector $\mathbf{x}$ therefore splits uniquely,
$$
\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_{\text{row}} + \mathbf{x}_{\text{null}},
\qquad \mathbf{x}_{\text{row}}\in C(A^\top),\ \ \mathbf{x}_{\text{null}}\in N(A),
$$
and the matrix ignores the null part entirely: $A\mathbf{x}=A\mathbf{x}_{\text{row}}+
A\mathbf{x}_{\text{null}}=A\mathbf{x}_{\text{row}}$. **The matrix only ever sees the
row-space part of its input.** That is the precise sense in which the row space is "what the
matrix acts on" and the null space is "what it is blind to" — and it is why the row space
maps one-to-one onto the column space (two inputs with the same row-space part give the same
output; two inputs with different row-space parts give different outputs, since their
difference is not in the null space). @fig-bigpic assembles the entire picture.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bigpic
#| fig-cap: 'The four fundamental subspaces, the way Strang draws them. **Left:** the input room $\mathbb{R}^n$, split into the row space (dim $r$) and the null space (dim $n-r$), which are perpendicular complements. **Right:** the output room $\mathbb{R}^m$, split into the column space (dim $r$) and the left null space (dim $m-r$), also perpendicular complements. The matrix $A$ maps the row space *one-to-one onto* the column space (solid arrow), and crushes the entire null space to the single point $\mathbf{0}$ (dashed arrow). Nothing ever lands in the left null space.'
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.8, 5.0))
ax.set_xlim(0, 10); ax.set_ylim(0, 8); ax.axis("off")

def box(cx, cy, w, h, color, label, sub):
    rect = plt.Rectangle((cx - w/2, cy - h/2), w, h, facecolor=color,
                         edgecolor="k", alpha=0.18, lw=1.4)
    ax.add_patch(rect)
    ax.text(cx, cy + 0.18, label, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=12,
            color=color, weight="bold")
    ax.text(cx, cy - 0.42, sub, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9.5, color="k")

# Input room (left).
ax.text(2.2, 7.5, r"input room  $\mathbb{R}^n$", ha="center", fontsize=12, weight="bold")
box(2.2, 5.6, 3.0, 1.4, HILITE, "row space", r"dim $= r$")
box(2.2, 2.4, 3.0, 1.4, ACCENT, "null space", r"dim $= n-r$")
ax.text(2.2, 4.0, r"$\perp$", ha="center", fontsize=15, color=GREY)

# Output room (right).
ax.text(7.8, 7.5, r"output room  $\mathbb{R}^m$", ha="center", fontsize=12, weight="bold")
box(7.8, 5.6, 3.0, 1.4, HILITE, "column space", r"dim $= r$")
box(7.8, 2.4, 3.0, 1.4, ACCENT, "left null space", r"dim $= m-r$")
ax.text(7.8, 4.0, r"$\perp$", ha="center", fontsize=15, color=GREY)

# The map.
ax.annotate("", xy=(6.3, 5.6), xytext=(3.7, 5.6),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=VEC, lw=2.6))
ax.text(5.0, 6.0, r"$A$: one-to-one onto", ha="center", fontsize=10.5, color=VEC)
ax.annotate("", xy=(7.8, 1.0), xytext=(3.7, 2.4),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GREY, lw=1.8, linestyle="--"))
ax.scatter([7.8], [1.0], color="k", s=40, zorder=6)
ax.text(6.1, 1.35, r"$A$: crush to $\mathbf{0}$", ha="center", fontsize=10, color=GREY)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the four-subspace machinery on real, honest data and matrices — never a toy rigged
to flatter. The first we carry through by hand; the others show the data meaning and the
edge of applicability.

## Example 1 — a rank-deficient matrix by hand {#sec-ex-byhand}

Take the matrix that motivated us, padded with one genuinely new direction so it is not
*entirely* degenerate:
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 4 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
The first two columns are parallel ($\mathbf{a}_2 = 2\mathbf{a}_1$), but the third,
$(0,1,3)$, points somewhere new. Let us find all four subspaces by hand and then verify
numerically.

**Column space and rank.** The columns are $\mathbf{a}_1=(1,2,-1)$,
$\mathbf{a}_2=(2,4,-2)$, $\mathbf{a}_3=(0,1,3)$. Since $\mathbf{a}_2=2\mathbf{a}_1$, the
column space is spanned by just $\mathbf{a}_1$ and $\mathbf{a}_3$. Are those two
independent? A combination $c_1\mathbf{a}_1+c_3\mathbf{a}_3=\mathbf{0}$ reads, in the first
coordinate, $c_1=0$; then the second gives $2(0)+c_3=0$ so $c_3=0$. Only the trivial
combination vanishes, so $\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_3$ are independent. Hence
$$
\operatorname{rank}(A) = 2, \qquad C(A)=\operatorname{span}\{(1,2,-1),\,(0,1,3)\}
\subseteq \mathbb{R}^3 ,
$$
a plane through the origin. The rank is $2$, not $3$: the matrix flattens 3-D space onto a
plane.

**Null space.** By rank–nullity (@thm-ranknullity), $\dim N(A)=n-r=3-2=1$ — a line. To find
it, solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$. The three scalar equations are
$$
x_1 + 2x_2 = 0,\qquad 2x_1 + 4x_2 + x_3 = 0,\qquad -x_1 - 2x_2 + 3x_3 = 0 .
$$
The first gives $x_1=-2x_2$. Substitute into the second:
$2(-2x_2)+4x_2+x_3 = -4x_2+4x_2+x_3 = x_3 = 0$, so $x_3=0$. Check the third with $x_3=0$:
$-(-2x_2)-2x_2+0 = 2x_2-2x_2 = 0$, satisfied for any $x_2$. So $x_2$ is free, $x_1=-2x_2$,
$x_3=0$, giving
$$
N(A) = \operatorname{span}\{(-2,\,1,\,0)\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3 .
$$
Notice $(-2,1,0)$ records exactly the column dependence $\mathbf{a}_2=2\mathbf{a}_1$:
feeding in $(-2,1,0)$ computes $-2\mathbf{a}_1+1\cdot\mathbf{a}_2+0\cdot\mathbf{a}_3 =
-2\mathbf{a}_1+2\mathbf{a}_1=\mathbf{0}$. **A null-space vector is a recipe for a vanishing
combination of columns** — that is what "the matrix is blind to this direction" means
concretely.

**Row space.** By @thm-rank-equal it also has dimension $2$. The rows are
$(1,2,0),(2,4,1),(-1,-2,3)$. Rows $1$ and $2$ are
independent (their third coordinates $0$ and $1$ cannot match by scaling row $1$), so
$$
C(A^\top) = \operatorname{span}\{(1,2,0),\,(2,4,1)\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3 .
$$
The orthogonality check of @thm-ortho must hold: $N(A)$'s generator $(-2,1,0)$ should be
perpendicular to both row-space basis vectors. Indeed $(-2,1,0)\cdot(1,2,0)=-2+2+0=0$ and
$(-2,1,0)\cdot(2,4,1)=-4+4+0=0$. The null line is exactly perpendicular to the row plane, as
the theorem promised.

**Left null space.** $\dim N(A^\top)=m-r=3-2=1$. It records the dependence among the
*rows*: a vector $\mathbf{y}=(y_1,y_2,y_3)$ with $A^\top\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{0}$ is exactly a
recipe $y_1\mathbf{r}_1+y_2\mathbf{r}_2+y_3\mathbf{r}_3=\mathbf{0}$ for a vanishing
combination of the rows $\mathbf{r}_1=(1,2,0)$, $\mathbf{r}_2=(2,4,1)$,
$\mathbf{r}_3=(-1,-2,3)$ (the row analogue of what the null space did for columns). We solve
it by hand. The transpose is
$$
A^\top = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & 4 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix},
$$
so $A^\top\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{0}$ is the three scalar equations
$$
y_1 + 2y_2 - y_3 = 0, \qquad 2y_1 + 4y_2 - 2y_3 = 0, \qquad y_2 + 3y_3 = 0 .
$$
The second equation is twice the first, so it carries no new information (the rows of
$A^\top$ are themselves dependent — its rank is $2$, as it must be). The third gives
$y_2=-3y_3$. Substitute into the first: $y_1 = -2y_2 + y_3 = -2(-3y_3)+y_3 = 7y_3$. So $y_3$
is free, and taking $y_3=1$,
$$
N(A^\top) = \operatorname{span}\{(7,\,-3,\,1)\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3 .
$$
Check that this combination of rows really vanishes:
$7(1,2,0)-3(2,4,1)+1(-1,-2,3) = (7-6-1,\ 14-12-2,\ 0-3+3) = (0,0,0)$, as promised. (The
numerical block below reports this same vector rescaled so its largest entry is $1$, i.e.
$(1,-\tfrac{3}{7},\tfrac{1}{7})\approx(1,-0.429,0.143)$.)

```{python}
#| label: tbl-ex1-check
#| echo: true
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[1, 2, 0],
              [2, 4, 1],
              [-1, -2, 3]], dtype=float)

r = np.linalg.matrix_rank(A)
print("rank(A)            =", r)                          # expect 2
# SVD: U columns span the output room, Vt rows span the input room.
U, s, Vt = np.linalg.svd(A)
tol = 1e-10
print("singular values    =", np.round(s, 6))

# Null space: right-singular vectors (rows of Vt) past the r-th -> basis of N(A).
nvec = Vt[r:].T[:, 0]
nvec = nvec / nvec[np.argmax(np.abs(nvec))]               # normalize for readability
print("null space basis   =", np.round(nvec, 3))
print("A @ (null vector)  =", np.round(A @ nvec, 12), " (should be 0)")

# Left null space: left-singular vectors (columns of U) past the r-th -> basis of N(A^T).
lvec = U[:, r:][:, 0]
lvec = lvec / lvec[np.argmax(np.abs(lvec))]
print("left null basis    =", np.round(lvec, 3))
print("(left null)^T @ A  =", np.round(lvec @ A, 12), " (should be 0)")
```

The computed null-space basis is a scalar multiple of $(-2,1,0)$ — matching our hand
calculation — and `A @ (null vector)` is zero to machine precision. The left null vector,
dotted into $A$ from the left, gives the all-zero row, confirming it records a row
dependence. (We used the SVD of [GEOM-20] only as a numerical microscope to *read off* the
subspaces; the math was done by hand above. The SVD is the tool that turns "find the four
subspaces" into a one-line computation, which is one reason it is the master decomposition.)

## Example 2 — a real data matrix with a redundant feature {#sec-ex-data}

Now the data reading, with realistic messiness. We build a design matrix of $n=200$ houses
with three features — *living area* (sq ft), *number of bedrooms*, and a third column that a
careless analyst added: *living area in square meters*. That third column is, up to
floating-point noise, $0.0929\times$ the first. The matrix *looks* like it carries three
features; it carries two.

```{python}
#| label: fig-data-rank
#| fig-cap: 'A design matrix with a phantom feature. **Left:** the singular-value spectrum (from the SVD) of the centered, standardized $200\times 3$ feature matrix. Three features, but the third singular value is essentially zero — the matrix is *numerically* rank 2. **Right:** the offending pair of columns; ''area in m$^2$'' is an exact rescaling of ''area in ft$^2$'', so they fall on a perfect line. The rank counts independent features: two, not three.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(2024)
n = 200
area_ft = rng.uniform(800, 3500, size=n)
bedrooms = np.clip(np.round(area_ft / 700 + rng.normal(0, 0.7, n)), 1, 6)
area_m2 = 0.092903 * area_ft            # the phantom feature: exact rescaling
X = np.column_stack([area_ft, bedrooms, area_m2])

# Standardize columns (zero mean, unit variance) so scales are comparable.
Xs = (X - X.mean(0)) / X.std(0)
U, s, Vt = np.linalg.svd(Xs, full_matrices=False)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.bar(["$\\sigma_1$", "$\\sigma_2$", "$\\sigma_3$"], s, color=[VEC, VEC, HILITE])
axL.set_title("Singular values of the feature matrix")
axL.set_ylabel("singular value")
for i, v in enumerate(s):
    axL.text(i, v + 0.4, f"{v:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=9)

axR.scatter(area_ft, area_m2, color=VEC, s=14, alpha=0.7)
axR.set_xlabel("living area (ft$^2$)"); axR.set_ylabel("living area (m$^2$)")
axR.set_title("Two columns, one direction")
plt.tight_layout()
print("numerical rank of standardized X =", np.linalg.matrix_rank(Xs))
print("singular values                  =", np.round(s, 4))
```

The third singular value is essentially zero, so the numerical rank is $2$: the matrix has
two features' worth of independent variation. The data lives in a $2$-dimensional flat
inside $\mathbb{R}^3$, and the third column adds nothing the first does not already carry.
This is the honest, common way rank-deficiency shows up — not as a designed example but as a
units mistake, a derived column, or a perfectly collinear pair. For a regression on this
$X$, the matrix $X^\top X$ is singular (its rank is also $2$, less than the $3$ coefficients
we want), and there is **no unique least-squares coefficient vector**: the null space of $X$
gives a whole line of coefficient vectors with identical fitted values. That is exactly the
breakdown the rank predicts, and it is the bridge to **Regression R5/R7**.

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: *near*-rank-deficiency {#sec-ex-edge}

The sharpest, most honest case is not exact rank-deficiency — which a computer detects
cleanly — but the *near* case, where columns are *almost* dependent. Here the rank is
mathematically full, yet the matrix behaves almost like a rank-deficient one, and "the
rank" stops being the right diagnostic. This is the edge where the rank concept strains and
you must reach for the **condition number** instead ([GEOM-26]).

```{python}
#| label: fig-near-rank
#| fig-cap: 'Where exact rank fails as a diagnostic. We tilt the phantom column away from exact collinearity by an angle $\theta$ and watch the smallest singular value $\sigma_{\min}$ (red, left axis) and the condition number $\kappa=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ (blue, right axis, log scale). The rank is *exactly 3* for every $\theta>0$ — the integer rank cannot see the difference between $\theta=10^{-4}$ and $\theta=1$. But $\kappa$ explodes as the columns near-align, and *that* is what predicts numerical disaster. Rank is a yes/no question; conditioning is the honest continuous one.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 200
angles = np.logspace(-4, 0, 40)   # radians from near-collinear to well-separated
v1 = rng.normal(size=n); v1 /= np.linalg.norm(v1)
w  = rng.normal(size=n); w -= (w @ v1) * v1; w /= np.linalg.norm(w)  # orthonormal to v1

ranks, smin, kappa = [], [], []
for th in angles:
    v2 = np.cos(th) * v1 + np.sin(th) * w   # column 2 at angle th from column 1
    third = rng.normal(size=n)              # a genuine third direction
    M = np.column_stack([v1, v2, third])
    s = np.linalg.svd(M, compute_uv=False)
    ranks.append(np.linalg.matrix_rank(M))
    smin.append(s[-1]); kappa.append(s[0] / s[-1])

fig, axL = plt.subplots()
axL.plot(angles, smin, color=HILITE, marker="o", ms=3, label=r"$\sigma_{\min}$")
axL.set_xscale("log"); axL.set_xlabel(r"separation angle $\theta$ (rad)")
axL.set_ylabel(r"$\sigma_{\min}$", color=HILITE)
axL.tick_params(axis="y", labelcolor=HILITE)
axR = axL.twinx()
axR.plot(angles, kappa, color=VEC, marker="s", ms=3, label=r"$\kappa$")
axR.set_yscale("log"); axR.set_ylabel(r"condition number $\kappa$", color=VEC)
axR.tick_params(axis="y", labelcolor=VEC); axR.grid(False)
axL.set_title(f"Exact rank is always {max(ranks)} here; conditioning tells the real story")
plt.tight_layout()
print("rank is constantly:", set(ranks), " across all angles")
```

The lesson the figure delivers — and the most important applicability judgement in this
article — is that **rank is an all-or-nothing integer, and real data almost never sits
exactly on the boundary.** Two columns that are $99.99\%$ aligned give a matrix of *full*
rank: the integer rank reports "all independent, nothing wrong," while the matrix is, for
every practical purpose, rank-deficient. The honest, continuous measure is the smallest
singular value $\sigma_{\min}$ (how close to dependent the columns are) and the condition
number $\kappa=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ (how much the near-dependence amplifies error).
This is precisely multicollinearity, and it is why **you never test for rank-deficiency by
computing a rank** on noisy data — you inspect the singular-value spectrum. We develop that
fully in [GEOM-26]; flag it now as the boundary where this article's clean integer gives
way to a number with a decimal point.

# Failure modes and what to reach for instead {#sec-failure}

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Numerical rank is not exact rank.** `numpy.linalg.matrix_rank` does *not* compute the true
algebraic rank — it counts singular values above a tolerance. On data with any noise, an
"almost dependent" column produces a tiny-but-nonzero singular value, and whether it counts
toward the rank depends entirely on the tolerance you (implicitly) chose. Two consequences:
(1) the reported rank of a real data matrix is a *thresholding decision*, not a fact about
the data; (2) the right question is almost never "what is the rank?" but "how small is the
smallest singular value, relative to the largest?" — i.e. the condition number ([GEOM-26]).
Treating the integer rank as ground truth on noisy data is the single most common mistake
this material invites.
:::

A few more honest boundaries:

- **Rank does not tell you *which* columns are redundant.** It is a single number. To find
  the offending dependence you need the null space (a null-space vector lists the
  coefficients of a vanishing column combination, as in @sec-ex-byhand) or, in practice, the
  SVD's right-singular vectors. If your goal is to *drop* a redundant feature, rank alone is
  the alarm, not the diagnosis.
- **Don't test invertibility with the determinant.** A square matrix is invertible iff it
  has full rank iff $\det\neq 0$ ([GEOM-11], [GEOM-13]) — true in exact arithmetic. But
  $\det$ is a terrible numerical singularity test: it scales like $\sigma_1\sigma_2\cdots
  \sigma_n$, so it can be tiny for a perfectly well-conditioned matrix (just small entries)
  or not-that-tiny for a nearly singular one. Use the smallest singular value / condition
  number, not $\det\approx 0$.
- **When the four subspaces are the wrong frame.** If you only need to *solve*
  $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ for a well-conditioned square $A$, you don't enumerate subspaces —
  you factor and back-substitute ([GEOM-11], and Cholesky in [GEOM-19] for the
  positive-definite case). The four-subspace picture earns its keep precisely when the matrix
  is rectangular or rank-deficient: it tells you *whether* a solution exists ($\mathbf{b}\in
  C(A)$?), *how many* there are (the size of $N(A)$), and — via the pseudoinverse of
  [GEOM-21] — which one to pick.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the rank the right thing to compute, and when is it not?"* The honest summary:

- **Use rank** for *exact* or symbolic matrices, for theoretical arguments (existence and
  uniqueness of solutions, dimension counting via rank–nullity), and as the conceptual lens
  for "how many independent directions are here." It is exactly right in exact arithmetic.
- **Do not lean on rank** for noisy, finite-precision data. There, the integer is a
  thresholded artifact; reach for the **singular-value spectrum** and the **condition
  number** ([GEOM-26]) for a continuous, honest measure of near-dependence, and the **SVD**
  ([GEOM-20]) or a **rank-revealing QR** ([GEOM-07]) to actually expose the structure.
- **The deepest connection to volunteer:** rank, the four subspaces, and the rank–nullity
  theorem are *read directly off the SVD* — the count of nonzero singular values is the rank,
  the corresponding left/right singular vectors are orthonormal bases for the column/row
  spaces, and the rest are bases for the two null spaces. So the SVD is not a separate
  topic; it is this article's four subspaces, made numerically robust.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-11 *Solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$*]**, which described the three
outcomes — unique solution, no solution, infinitely many — and hinted that each is governed
by a subspace. This article named those subspaces and proved how they fit together: one
rank $r$, the rank–nullity accounting $r+(n-r)=n$, and the two perpendicular pairs. From
here the track continues:

- **[GEOM-13 *The Determinant*]** — the single number for square matrices that detects
  full rank ($\det\neq 0$) geometrically, as a volume-scaling factor; and the honest reason
  it is a *bad* numerical singularity test.
- **[GEOM-14]–[GEOM-15] *Eigenvalues and Diagonalization*]** — special directions a matrix
  only scales; the null space is the eigenspace for eigenvalue $0$, so rank-deficiency is
  "the matrix has a zero eigenvalue."
- **[GEOM-20 *The Singular Value Decomposition*]** — the master factorization that reads
  *all four subspaces at once* off orthonormal bases, makes the rank the count of nonzero
  singular values, and turns the near-rank-deficiency of @sec-ex-edge into a precise,
  continuous statement.
- **[GEOM-26 *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*]** — the continuous successor to
  the integer rank, $\kappa=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$, which is the honest diagnostic for
  the *near*-rank-deficient matrices real data produces.

Downstream of the track, this article is the linear-algebra core behind **Regression R5**
(when $X^\top X$ is invertible — exactly when $X$ has full column rank) and **Regression
R7** (multicollinearity is near-rank-deficiency of the design matrix).
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The canonical source.** Gilbert Strang, *Introduction to Linear Algebra* and *Linear
  Algebra and Its Applications* — the four-fundamental-subspaces framing, the "big picture"
  diagram of @fig-bigpic, and the insistence on rank–nullity as a single conservation law
  are all his. His essay "The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra" (*American Mathematical
  Monthly*, 1993) collects the four-subspace orthogonality into one statement.
- **The factorization proof.** The $A=CF$ argument for row rank = column rank in
  @thm-rank-equal is the *rank factorization* (or *full-rank factorization*); it is the
  cleanest proof and the conceptual seed of the SVD's $A=U\Sigma V^\top$, where the same
  rank $r$ appears as the number of nonzero singular values.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How do you compute the subspaces stably?* — via
  the SVD, [GEOM-20]. *What is the "right" rank of a noisy matrix?* — there isn't a crisp
  answer; you trade off approximation error against rank, the Eckart–Young story of
  [GEOM-21]. *Why does a zero eigenvalue mean rank-deficiency?* — because the eigenvector for
  $\lambda=0$ lives in the null space, developed in [GEOM-14].
